text_blocks.text: Raymond B. Bragg, the associate editor of The New Humanist,
sought to consolidate the input of Leon Milton Birkhead, Charles Francis
Potter, and several members of the Western Unitarian Conference. Bragg
asked Roy Wood Sellars to draft a document based on this
information which resulted in the publication of the Humanist Manifesto
in 1933. Potter's book and the Manifesto became the cornerstones of
modern humanism, the latter declaring a new religion by saying, "any
religion that can hope to be a synthesising and dynamic force for today
must be shaped for the needs of this age. To establish such a religion is
a major necessity of the present." It then presented 15 theses of
humanism as foundational principles for this new religion.
